results.possibleIdentity: Marilyn Perlman (1925–2010) was an accomplished artist based in New York, active during the late 1960s, 1970s, and into the 1980s. Her work was included in the Art Lending Service (ALS) and Art Advisory Service (AAS) at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) between 1973 and 1974. These services were designed to promote contemporary artists by renting and selling their works to individuals and corporations. She had a notable exhibition titled "Continuous Heavens" which ran from June 29 to August 18, 1985. Evidence of her career is preserved in the archives of the prominent art critic Nicolas Calas, who was a significant figure in the New York art scene and closely involved with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Perlman lived in New York City and later in Upper Montclair, New Jersey, and passed away on November 23, 2010, at the age of 85.

results.possibleIdentity: Clifford Hallett Glynn, Jr. (known as Cliff Glynn) was a New York-based artist, sculptor, and designer active during the mid-20th century. Born on July 8, 1916, in Leominster, Massachusetts, he later settled in Manhattan where he pursued a multi-faceted career. In 1958, he served as the first vice president of the Artists Guild of New York and worked as a freelance designer specializing in promotion. By the late 1960s and early 1970s, Glynn was recognized as a sculptor and artist, working with found materials, metal, clay, and wood, as well as producing paintings and drawings in media such as watercolors and pastels. His work was reviewed in the mid-1960s by art critics like Michael Benedikt and appeared in contexts alongside trends in modern French painting. In 1974, he registered a copyright for 'Cliff Glynn corporate graphics,' reflecting his ongoing work in design. He was married to Eunice Geraldine Blum until the early 1970s and later married Marilyn Fish in 1983. He continued his artistic practice until his death on August 9, 1996, in New York City. While his name is frequently associated with late 1960s technology and engineering due to the prominence of NASA flight directors Cliff Charlesworth and Glynn Lunney during the Apollo missions, the historical artist Cliff Glynn was a distinct figure in the New York art and design community of that era.
